columns: Method note: adaptation of Frey & Osborne + Anthropic labor-market exposure framework

rows.Method note: adaptation of Frey & Osborn: 1. Core idea
Scores combine two layers:
- A Frey-Osborne-style long-run replaceability layer based on task bottlenecks: perception/manipulation, creative intelligence, and social intelligence.
- An Anthropic-style AI coverage layer that separates theoretical AI coverage from observed AI coverage in current real-world usage.
2. Added output columns
- old_frey_osborne_style_score: estimated classic computerisation score using task bottlenecks only.
- theoretical_ai_coverage: estimated share of tasks that modern LLM-centered systems could plausibly perform or accelerate in principle.
- observed_ai_coverage: estimated share of tasks that appear plausibly covered in real market deployment today.
- probability_of_computerisation: updated long-run occupation-level replacement probability.
- speed_of_replacement_score: estimated pace of practical replacement given adoption friction.
3. Scoring logic
- Job title and description are both used. If a title is vague, description matters more.

- Features inferred from the text include: digital/task structure, repetition, decomposability, physical presence, creativity, social trust, regulation/liability, workflow automation exposure, robotics exposure, and economic incentive.
- Long-run replacement is scored at the occupation level, not mere task assistance.
- Speed is scored separately from long-run replaceability.
4. Interpretation
- High probability + low speed = automatable in principle, but blocked by trust, regulation, hardware, or adoption friction.
- Medium probability + high speed = a large portion of the role is already being displaced quickly, even if full substitution is incomplete.
5. Limits
- This is a structured heuristic exercise, not Anthropic's proprietary task-level measurement and not the original paper's classifier trained on hand-labeled occupations.
- No direct task-frequency weighting from O*NET task statements was used here; the occupation description is a proxy.
- Scores should be interpreted as calibrated estimates for prioritization, not precise forecasts.
Key source URLs:
https://www.anthropic.com/research/labor-market-impacts
https://www.anthropic.com/news/anthropic-economic-index
https://arxiv.org/abs/2303.10130
https://www.oxfordmartin.ox.ac.uk/publications/the-future-of-employment

columns: Work like a labor economist + automation researcher, not like a futurist marketer. Prefer hard-nosed task analysis over broad narratives.

rows.Work like a labor economist + automation: 1) a research paper: Frey & Osborne, The Future of Employment
2) an XLSX with job titles and descriptions
3) Anthropic's Labor market impacts of AI report as the modern update layer
For every occupation in the XLSX, score the job using BOTH frameworks:
A) a Frey-Osborne-style long-run computerisation score based on task bottlenecks
B) an Anthropic-style AI coverage layer that distinguishes theoretical capability from observed real-world adoption
Return the full table with these columns:
- old_frey_osborne_style_score: 0.00-1.00 estimate of classic computerisation risk using bottlenecks around perception/manipulation, creative intelligence, and social intelligence.
- theoretical_ai_coverage: 0.00-1.00 estimate of the share of job tasks that modern LLM-centered systems could theoretically perform or materially accelerate.
- observed_ai_coverage: 0.00-1.00 estimate of the share of job tasks already plausibly covered in practice by current AI deployment; this should usually be less than or equal to theoretical coverage.
- probability_of_computerisation: 0.00-1.00 estimate of long-run occupation-level replacement, updated for modern AI and robotics.
- speed_of_replacement_score: 0.00-1.00 estimate of how quickly practical replacement is likely to happen in the real world.
Do NOT confuse long-run replaceability with near-term rollout.
A job can have high probability_of_computerisation but low speed_of_replacement_score if adoption is blocked by regulation, trust, hardware limits, workflow complexity, liability, or weak ROI.
A job can have medium probability_of_computerisation but high speed_of_replacement_score if many tasks are already being displaced quickly even though full substitution is incomplete.
- data structure and digital nature of the work
- ease of decomposition into automatable subtasks
- evidence that replacement is already underway
- theoretical_ai_coverage should be high when work is digital, text-heavy, analytical, codified, and easy to quality-check.
- observed_ai_coverage should be high only when current deployment is already visible and integration barriers are low.
- old_frey_osborne_style_score should reflect classical bottlenecks even if adoption is not happening yet.
- probability_of_computerisation should score the occupation as a whole, not whether some tasks can be assisted.
- speed_of_replacement_score should reflect practical rollout, not just technical possibility.
- Very High = already happening rapidly now
- High = likely in the next few years
- why the old Frey-Osborne score is what it is
- why theoretical and observed coverage differ
- why long-run probability is what it is
- why speed is what it is
- If title and description conflict, say so and use best judgment.
- Be skeptical of full replacement claims when the remaining human tasks are central to the occupation.
- Do not assign extreme scores casually.
